**Subjective**
**Chief Complaint**
Acute low back pain

**History of Present Illness**
Mr. Okonkwo is a 43-year-old gentleman presenting with acute low back pain. The pain began four days ago, on Saturday, while helping his brother-in-law move furniture. He felt a pulling sensation in his lower back while lifting a dresser. He had to stop and sit down at the time of the injury. He continued to help but the pain worsened, so he stopped assisting. The pain is described as across the low back, worse on the right, with radiation into the right buttock and occasionally into the upper thigh, but not below the knee. He denies numbness, tingling, weakness, difficulty lifting his foot, tripping, or leg buckling. He denies any bladder or bowel changes. He denies fever. He has a history of occasional mild back soreness with yard work, but nothing like this current episode. The pain is currently about a five or six out of ten at rest, spiking to an eight with transitions like standing up from a chair or getting out of bed in the morning. He has been taking ibuprofen 400 milligrams (two 200 mg tablets) three times a day, and using a heating pad. He states the ibuprofen takes the edge off but does not fully relieve it.

**Review of Systems**
Constitutional: [Fever]
HEENT: [Vision, hearing, throat, nasal symptoms]
Cardiovascular: [Chest pain, palpitations, edema]
Respiratory: [Shortness of breath, cough, wheezing]

Musculoskeletal: [Joint pain, muscle weakness, mobility issues]
Neurological: [Headache, dizziness, numbness, weakness]
Psychiatric: [Mood, anxiety, sleep]
Skin: [Rashes, lesions, wounds]

**Subjective**
**Chief Complaint**
Acute low back pain

**History of Present Illness**
Mr. Okonkwo is a 43-year-old gentleman presenting with acute low back pain. The pain began four days ago, on Saturday, while helping his brother-in-law move furniture. He felt a pulling sensation in his lower back while lifting a dresser. He had to stop and sit down at the time of the injury. He continued to help but the pain worsened, so he stopped assisting. The pain is described as across the low back, worse on the right, with radiation into the right buttock and occasionally into the upper thigh, but not below the knee. He denies numbness, tingling, weakness, difficulty lifting his foot, tripping, or leg buckling. He denies any bladder or bowel changes. He denies fever. He has a history of occasional mild back soreness with yard work, but nothing like this current episode. The pain is currently about a five or six out of ten at rest, spiking to an eight with transitions like standing up from a chair or getting out of bed in the morning. He has been taking ibuprofen 400 milligrams (two 200 mg tablets) three times a day, and using a heating pad. He states the ibuprofen takes the edge off but does not fully relieve it.

**Subjective**
**Chief Complaint**
Acute low back pain

**History of Present Illness**
Mr. Okonkwo is a 43-year-old gentleman presenting with acute low back pain. The pain began four days ago, on Saturday, while helping his brother-in-law move furniture. He felt a pulling sensation in his lower back while lifting a dresser. He had to stop and sit down at the time of the injury. He continued to help but the pain worsened, so he stopped assisting. The pain is described as across the low back, worse on the right, with radiation into the right buttock and occasionally into the upper thigh, but not below the knee. He denies numbness, tingling, weakness, difficulty lifting his foot, tripping, or leg buckling. He denies any bladder or bowel changes. He denies fever. He has a history of occasional mild back soreness with yard work, but nothing like this current episode. The pain is currently about a five or six out of ten at rest, spiking to an eight with transitions like standing up from a chair or getting out of bed in the morning. He has been taking ibuprofen 400 milligrams (two 200 mg tablets) three times a day, and using a heating pad. He states the ibuprofen takes the edge off but does not fully relieve it.
